stem: What does the CPU (Central Processing Unit) do?

options.text: Stores files permanently on your computer
Connects your computer to the internet
Executes program instructions one at a time, very quickly

explanation_after: The CPU is the brain — it fetches instructions, decodes them, and executes them. Modern CPUs do this billions of times per second (measured in GHz). It handles sequential logic: step 1, step 2, step 3. The key word is 'sequentially' — which is why CPUs are powerful for general tasks but not optimal for AI training.

stem: Why are GPUs used for training neural networks instead of CPUs?

options.text: GPUs have faster clock speeds than CPUs
GPUs can run thousands of simple operations simultaneously (parallel processing)

explanation_after: Training a neural network means doing the same matrix multiply operation on thousands of values simultaneously — these operations are completely independent of each other. A CPU has 8-32 cores (fast, sequential). A GPU has 10,000+ simpler cores that all work in parallel. For independent batch operations, 10,000 slow cores beat 32 fast ones every time.

prompt: A CPU is optimised for ______ tasks. A GPU is optimised for ______ tasks.
explanation_after: Sequential = one thing at a time, very fast. Parallel = many things at once, each slower but combined much faster for suitable workloads. Neural network training is a parallel workload — millions of independent multiplications.
